name: Vancouver
headline: Municipal Plant-Based Procurement Review Motion
achievements: Directed staff to evaluate plant-based procurement opportunities to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and costs
Reviewed procurement scenarios proposed by the Vancouver Humane Society
Initiated ongoing work on lower-emission food procurement practices and food-related emissions tracking
Linked municipal food purchasing to climate action objectives

name: Toronto
headline: City Food Procurement Tied to a 25% Emissions-Reduction Goal
summary: Toronto is one of Canada’s strongest municipal examples of linking institutional meals to a quantified climate target.
achievements: Committed to reducing greenhouse-gas emissions associated with food procured by the City by 25% by 2030.
Measures the climate impact of meals served through municipal operations.
Uses planetary-health and plant-forward approaches to identify lower-carbon food choices.
Applied the commitment to corporate food procurement rather than limiting action to public education.

image.alt: Toronto City Hall and Nathan Phillips Square

name: Halton Region
headline: Municipal Plant-Forward Motion
achievements: Evaluate increasing plant-based food options at Regional facilities and events
Align food offerings with Canada’s Food Guide
Support reducing food-related climate impacts
Promote food waste reduction strategies

image.alt: Aerial view of Halton Region near Lion's Valley Park in Oakville

name: Kingston
headline: Plant-Based Motion Passed
achievements: Prioritize increasing plant-rich and plant-based food procurement at City of Kingston facilities and events
Support healthy, sustainable, and lower-carbon food choices in alignment with Canada’s Food Guide and climate objectives
Encourage awareness and education regarding the environmental benefits of plant-based eating
Work collaboratively with community organizations and stakeholders to advance sustainable food initiatives
Support reductions in food-related greenhouse gas emissions through municipal food policies and practices

image.alt: Waterfront and cityscape in Kingston, Ontario

name: Montréal
headline: Plant-Forward Motion Passed May 16, 2022
summary: Motion Unanimous 65 Councillors Municipal Plant-Forward Motion That the City of Montréal May 16, 2022
achievements: Adopted the C40 Good Food Cities Declaration.
Committed to serving at least 75% vegetarian food at City-organized events.
Committed to aligning municipal food procurement with the Planetary Health Diet.
Supported increased plant-based food consumption across Montréal.
Directed work with municipal food-service concessionaires to increase plant-based offerings.
organizationsInvolved: Sustainable Food Transition Coalition & Plant-Based Cities Movement

name: Government of Canada / Health Canada
headline: Canada’s National Food Guide Prioritizes Plant Protein
summary: The Food Guide is a foundational national policy accomplishment that supports institutional plant-forward action across Canada.
achievements: Recommends choosing protein foods that come from plants more often.
Places beans, lentils, nuts, seeds, tofu and other plant proteins within the core national healthy-eating framework.
Recognizes that food choices can affect both personal health and the environment.
Gives public institutions a strong federal evidence base for increasing plant-based meals and revising procurement standards.

image.alt: Canada's food guide plate featuring vegetables, fruits, whole grains and protein foods

name: Vancouver General Hospital
headline: 2024 Plant-Rich Menu Pilot
achievements: Piloted more than 20 new plant-rich menu items for patients
Increased availability of nutritious, lower-carbon meal choices
Supported patient health, recovery, and environmental sustainability goals
Demonstrated how healthcare food services can reduce their climate impact while maintaining nutritional quality
Led by Vancouver Coastal Health as part of its planetary health and sustainability initiatives

image.alt: Plant-rich patient meal trays developed for Vancouver General Hospital's menu pilot

name: University of Guelph Child Care and Learning Centre
headline: Fully Plant-Based Child-Care Menu Introduced in 2020

stats.label: Reported relative reduction in monthly menu-related carbon footprint

summary: The University of Guelph Child Care and Learning Centre switched to a fully plant-based menu in 2020. A Foodsteps assessment reported that the change reduced the menu’s monthly carbon footprint by nearly 65%, saving more than 1,900 kg CO2e per month compared with the previous meat-and-dairy menu.
achievements: Introduced a fully plant-based menu for meals and snacks in 2020.
Removed meat, dairy and eggs from the centre’s regular menu.
Reduced the reported monthly menu-related carbon footprint by nearly 65%.
Saved an estimated 1,900 kg CO2e per month compared with the previous menu.
Improved meal inclusion and simplified accommodation of dietary restrictions and allergies.
organizationsInvolved: University of Guelph Child Care and Learning Centre

image.alt: Plant-based lentil shepherd's pie portions prepared at the University of Guelph Child Care and Learning Centre

name: University of Calgary
headline: Thousands of Plant-Forward Recipes and a Dedicated Vegan Residence Station
summary: The program combines permanent vegan service, lower-carbon menu identification and large-scale recipe development rather than relying on occasional specials.
achievements: Operates a dedicated vegan station within the university’s principal residence dining program.
Incorporates Coolfood meals that identify dishes meeting a recognized lower-carbon standard.
Gives culinary teams access to more than 3,000 plant-forward recipes, including more than 2,000 vegan recipes.
Reports that 20% of food purchasing is sustainably certified.

image.alt: Plant-forward ingredients and dishes at University of Calgary Food Services

name: University of British Columbia
headline: Climate-Friendly Food Procurement Guidelines Support a 50% Food-Emissions Target
summary: UBC adopted climate-friendly food-system procurement guidelines under its Climate Action Plan 2030. The guidelines support a university target to reduce food-system greenhouse-gas emissions by 50% by 2030, including reductions in ruminant meat and dairy purchases and increased use of climate-friendly alternatives.
achievements: Adopted climate-friendly food-system procurement guidelines for campus food providers.
Set a target to reduce food-system greenhouse-gas emissions by 50% by 2030.
Directed providers to establish baselines and reduction targets for ruminant meat and dairy purchases.
Promoted substitutions with climate-friendly alternatives, including plant-based milk and lower-carbon menu options.

name: University of Winnipeg
headline: Sprout Converted to a 100% Plant-Based Campus Food Outlet
summary: This is one of Canada’s clearest examples of a university converting an established outlet to an entirely plant-based model.
achievements: Replaced Sprout’s previous menu with a completely plant-based food program.
Developed new dishes with Forward Food culinary support and Diversity Food Services.
Created a permanent campus destination where every menu item is plant-based.
Demonstrated that a fully plant-based operation can function within mainstream university food service.

image.alt: Chef Jessica Young in the University of Winnipeg kitchen ahead of the Sprout plant-based menu launch

name: Dalhousie University
headline: Plant-Based Baseline Established and 50% Menu Goal Set for 2030
summary: Dalhousie pairs a published baseline with a long-term target, creating a clear framework for measuring progress.
achievements: Measured a baseline showing approximately 33% of food purchases by dollar value were plant-based.
Set an objective for at least 50% of menu options to be plant-based by 2030.
Introduced Cool Food identification to help students select lower-carbon meals.
Integrated procurement, menu planning, health and food-related emissions into a ten-year institutional food plan.

image.alt: Sweet potato and quinoa bowl served through Dalhousie campus dining

name: University of Toronto Mississauga
headline: Additional 20% of Campus Menus Committed to Plant-Based Food
summary: UTM’s pledge extends a measurable plant-based menu commitment across a second major University of Toronto campus.
achievements: Joined the Forward Food Pledge alongside the St. George campus.
Committed to converting an additional 20% of campus menus to plant-based offerings.
Supported the commitment with hands-on culinary training designed for high-volume institutional food service.
Expanded plant-based dining as a campus sustainability action rather than a single-location initiative.

image.alt: University of Toronto and Forward Food chefs training in a campus kitchen

name: University of Toronto – St. George Campus
shortName: University of Toronto – St. George Campus
headline: Additional 20% of Campus Menus Committed to Plant-Based Food
summary: The pledge established a measurable expansion commitment at one of Canada’s largest university campuses.
achievements: Signed the Forward Food Pledge after intensive plant-based culinary training for campus chefs.
Committed to converting an additional 20% of campus menus to plant-based offerings, beyond the existing baseline.
Built staff capacity to produce appealing, culturally diverse and scalable plant-based meals.
Connected menu transformation with the university’s broader sustainability and food-emissions work.

image.alt: University of Toronto chefs preparing plant-based dishes during Forward Food training

name: McGill University
headline: Plant-Based Food Embedded in McGill’s Climate and Sustainability Strategy
summary: The strategy established plant-based dining as a measurable part of McGill’s institutional sustainability work.
achievements: Made expanded plant-based options an official sustainable-food objective.
Improved institutional tracking of plant-based food purchases in annual sustainability reporting.
Connected dining changes with responsible sourcing, climate action and McGill’s STARS performance.
Created a university-level accountability framework for plant-based purchasing rather than relying solely on individual vendors.

image.alt: Students tending a campus food garden at McGill University

name: Université Laval
headline: Nearly 40% of Campus Food Purchases Are Plant-Origin
summary: Université Laval combines procurement measurement with a practical framework for designing lower-carbon meals.
achievements: Developed an “eco-efficient meal” method that evaluates both nutritional quality and greenhouse-gas impact.
Asked campus food operators to offer meals with stronger nutrition and lower climate impact.
Reports that nearly 40% of food-service purchases are foods of plant origin.
Embedded healthy, plant-forward and local food into its Horizon 2030 sustainability direction.

image.alt: Food service staff preparing meals at a Université Laval campus dining counter
